channel.title: Rob Burbea Guided Meditations
channel.description: A companion feed of Rob Burbea guided meditations, guided practice sessions, and retreat instructions from Dharma Seed.
channel.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
channel.itunes:summary: A companion feed of Rob Burbea guided meditations, guided practice sessions, and retreat instructions from Dharma Seed.

item.title: Metta Practice, and a few things about Piti (Instructions)
item.description:
The talk begins by distinguishing two intentions in metta practice: cultivating love for its own sake, or using metta as a springboard into samadhi and jhana. From there, the emphasis shifts to sensitivity—how to adjust phrases, volume, spacing, and attention so the practice can open into piti, the body-based pleasure or well-being that may arise in many different ways. The second half briefly defines piti, then lays out how it can appear through concentration, open-heartedness, or simple appreciation, and how to work with it once it is there by spreading it through the body and alternating between narrow probing attention and open, receptive enjoyment.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the frame 00:57 Metta as springboard 04:56 Attunement and phrases 10:22 From metta to joy 11:51 What piti is 14:19 How piti arises 19:30 Let it pervade 21:04 Two modes of attention 25:07 Practice at the edge
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60871/

item.title: Breathing with the Energy Body (Guided Meditation)
item.description:
A guided exploration of the energy body begins with posture, then widens into whole-body awareness, asking how the breath affects texture, tone, vibration, and spaciousness. The practice keeps returning to a gentle, comfortable breath and then tours different points in the body—navel, solar plexus, throat, crown, heart, sacrum—checking what ripples out from each. Near the end, the talk steps back to clarify that there is no single right sequence, only experimentation with what supports well-being and samadhi.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the posture 02:50 Whole-body awareness 05:38 Long breath and expansion 09:32 Breath at the navel 13:14 Moving through the midline 17:53 Heart and throat tour 23:46 Crown, neck, and back 34:07 Sacrum and release 37:20 Practice reflections
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60862/

item.title: The Energy Body and the Whole-body breath (Instructions and Guided Meditation)
item.description:
How does the breath open the whole body without becoming strained or coarse? The talk first defines what is meant by the energy body and whole-body awareness, then moves into a guided meditation that starts with posture, opens attention to the whole body, and follows the longest comfortable breath as it expands, contracts, energizes, and relaxes the space. Afterward, the speaker recaps the practice and widens it into daily life: staying light, responsive, and willing to experiment rather than falling back on default habits.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the approach 02:41 Whole body and energy 05:06 A teaching story 12:04 Posture and presence 18:46 Breath in the whole body 27:05 Solar plexus play 34:02 What feels best 40:17 Review and principle 47:59 Daily life practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60884/

item.title: Counting Within the Breath (Guided Meditation)
item.description:
What changes when you count not before the breath or after it, but inside the breath itself? The talk introduces a guided practice that matches the count to the length of the in-breath and out-breath, while keeping whole-body awareness open and alive, and then shifts into the details of how to make the practice comfortable, bright, and workable. After the guided section, the speaker explains why this method can steady attention, counter low energy, and even support the approach to first jhana, before giving practical retreat guidance on posture, movement, and interviews.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:45 Counting Within Breath 29:27 Settling After Practice 30:31 Why It Helps 31:57 Attention and Jhana 33:48 Posture and Comfort 35:48 Movement and Energy 37:21 Interviews and Practice Focus 40:10 Practical Questions
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60863/

item.title: Intention and Devotion (Guided Meditation)
item.description:
What if meditation is not just about settling down, but about sensing where life wants to open into sacredness? The talk moves from the contrast between simplifying practices and the more complex, generative movement of imaginal practice, then turns into a guided exercise of standing, walking, sensing the body, and letting intention become a quiet devotion. It closes by emphasizing that intention is impermanent and must be returned to gently, like tending a flame, while care for the heart and body is kept alive in movement.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Renunciation and desire 02:17 Imaginal complexity 05:49 The body becomes sacred 09:39 Movement and stillness 14:24 Intention toward sacredness 16:07 Standing exercise 21:36 Walking with holy fire 29:36 Returning to the flame 34:30 Bowing and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43942/

item.title: Eros in the Imaginal (Guided Meditation)
item.description:
How do you work with an image that is more than visual, more than personal, and more than a tool for metta? The talk first carefully sets out what counts as imaginal: something sensed in relationship, with resonance, mystery, autonomy, beauty, and even a quality of sacredness, rather than a merely imagined picture. It then moves into a long guided meditation that begins with the energy body, invites an image to arise or be chosen, and explores different kinds of contact—gazing, identifying, witnessing, and the possibility of erotic or other forms of communion—before ending with practical ways to enter imaginal work from dreams, emotion, the body, or the room itself.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What Imaginal Means 10:01 Settling Into Practice 11:12 Energy Body and Samadhi 19:49 Letting An Image Form 23:41 Ways Of Relating 29:29 Eros And Transformation 37:19 Primary Sense And Resonance 48:25 Choosing And Receiving Images
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43938/

item.title: Images of the Breath: Energy Body, Colour, and the Beloved (Guided Meditation)
item.description:
The talk begins with a simple question: what changes when breath is felt not just as air in the body, but as energy, image, and field? It moves slowly through several entry points — lower belly, feet, back, heart, crown, and front of the body — while repeatedly inviting the listener to notice what feels good, subtle, or right rather than forcing a fixed method. From there it opens into light and color, then into the possibility of an imaginal beloved, so that breath becomes a way of sensing relationship, blessing, and the felt shape of the world.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why images matter 03:06 Settling the body 06:51 Lower centers and grounding 11:23 Heart and crown 14:32 Front of body opening 20:08 Porous field of breath 23:51 Light and color 29:16 Blessing radiance 34:14 Meeting the beloved
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37009/

item.title: Guided Meditation 1
item.description:
The practice begins with posture and a quiet body scan, softening tension in the face, throat, shoulders, belly, and hands before widening attention to the whole seated field. From there, the talk keeps returning to a simple movement: open the awareness again and again, feel the body as an energy space, and notice what helps that space feel bright, relaxed, and alive. It then introduces optional images—lines of energy through the center and down the legs, the breath as a gentle expansion and contraction, and even the body as translucent light—while reminding listeners to keep only what feels useful.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the posture 01:18 Releasing tension 02:53 Grounding in contact 03:48 Opening the field 05:37 Restoring spacious presence 09:02 Imagining energy lines 11:43 Breath and support 15:18 Experimenting with form 19:00 Body of light
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/27488/

item.title: Body/Image - A Guided Meditation
item.description:
What happens if the body is not just felt from within, but contemplated as an image, an element, and even a question about solidity itself? The meditation begins with grounding in the whole body and a steady frame of kindness, then moves through anatomy, the four elements, and reflections on space and matter, gradually loosening the sense of a hard boundary between self and world. In the later part, the talk becomes more imaginal, using the image of a body drawn from its sheath and a radiant, mind-made body floating free before returning to stillness and integration.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 02:41 Settling In 03:32 Practice as Kindness 06:34 Exploring the Lower Body 16:39 Pelvis and Digestive System 24:23 Head and Nervous System 28:38 Elements and Interbeing 35:35 Solidity and Mystery 41:51 Luminous Body 51:00 Integration
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/14583/

item.title: Working with the Emotional Body (Instructions and Guided Meditation: Day Seven)
item.description:
How do emotions arise, and what changes when we stop treating them as fixed things? The talk moves between several ways of working with emotional life: seeing an inner critic as a character or as passing thoughts, simplifying experience down to body sensation, and noticing how stories and self-views can lock feeling into place. It also points to quieter moments in meditation, where emotion is seen beginning to form, and asks whether we have the courage both to feel difficult states and to let them go quiet.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 One Perspective Among Many 01:19 Inner Dynamics and Characters 03:19 Stories and Simplicity 05:18 How Emotion Forms 07:42 What Fabrication Gives 10:56 Self-View and Looping 14:12 Courage to Feel 17:05 Resources Becoming Accessible
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13854/

item.title: Working With The Emotional Body (Instructions and Guided Meditation: Day Six)
item.description:
The talk starts with a guided meditation that keeps returning to posture, groundedness, and a careful kind of kindness toward whatever is most prominent in experience. From there, it moves into a sustained teaching on allowing: drawing close to sensations and feelings, softening around them, and seeing how much suffering comes from reactivity rather than the raw experience itself. It then widens into anger and fear, the second arrow, and finally a more unusual claim that experience itself is shaped by relationship and by the way the mind looks at it, with a brief shift into emptiness and doership. The closing turns practical again, with suggestions for language like welcoming, embracing, or letting be, and a reminder to find what actually works in practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Settling and kindness 03:21 Meeting what is prominent 08:17 Allowing as practice 15:15 Softening and letting go 24:45 Anger and fear 32:23 The second arrow 35:09 Relationship shapes experience 41:27 Letting be, not control 46:07 Doership and closing advice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13855/

item.title: Working with the Emotional Body (Instructions and Guided Meditation: Day Four)
item.description:
How does a painful feeling change when it is held in a larger field? The talk moves from brief review of earlier emotional-body practices into a long guided meditation that starts with upright, soft posture, then opens from the center of the body to the whole body, to sound, silence, and spacious awareness. In the second half, the discussion returns to questions about fear, stillness, and why expanding the context can make contraction, pain, or thought feel less compelling; a Buddha salt-crystal metaphor makes that point concrete. The talk closes with practical suggestions for walking, nature, and community as ways to keep the field open.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Review and orientation 03:32 Posture and kindness 08:57 Centering the body 13:01 Opening to sound 19:35 Space and letting go 26:10 Including contraction 35:05 Discussion and salt crystal 43:45 Choosing the larger context
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13852/

item.title: Working with the Emotional Body (Instructions and Guided Meditation: Day Three)
item.description:
How do emotions change when attention stops arguing with them and starts noticing the mind’s commentary as part of the field? The talk moves from reviewing a body-based practice, to exploring labeling and how naming can shape experience, to opening the whole body as a wider field that can sometimes be bathed in ease and pleasure. From there it turns to the mind’s judgments, assumptions, and self-conclusions, then closes with short experiments: seeing whether a thought like “this is exactly right” changes the feel of practice, and whether briefly setting aside past and future can lighten the load of an emotion.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Reviewing the approach 04:07 The energy body 06:33 Feeling and enjoyment 08:24 Mind's commentary 14:46 Short practice experiments 20:09 A supportive view 24:18 Unweighting the moment 28:53 Quickness and kindness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13856/

item.title: Working with the Emotional Body (Instructions and Guided Meditation: Day Two)
item.description:
How do you meet tiredness, pain, and emotion without turning them into one solid mass? The talk moves from practical instructions on sloth, torpor, and physical discomfort into a guided exploration of attention: first along a central line in the body, then as a wider “bubble” of awareness that can hold experience with more space and less strain. A passage on Cézanne’s patient looking briefly frames the teaching, which returns again and again to curiosity, softness, and trying what actually helps in the moment.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Introducing the approach 07:03 Tiredness and aversion 09:58 Working with discomfort 13:54 Softness and detail 20:39 Cézanne and patience 24:15 Guided central line practice 33:51 Expanding the field 40:30 Inquiry and resourcing 50:51 Two practices to explore
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13857/

item.title: Working with the Emotional Body (Instructions and Guided Meditation: Day One)
item.description:
What if an emotion is not something to solve, but something the body can hold long enough to understand? The talk begins by framing citta as the heart-mind, then moves through the range of emotions, mind states, and the ways distraction and expectation can tighten them further. From there it settles into a guided practice: feeling the body, noticing where an emotion shows up, and letting attention meet it with enough steadiness to make it less overwhelming. A repeated image of waves at the seaside gives the teaching its shape: what rises changes, and awareness can learn not to drown in it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Heart-Mind Framing 04:33 What We Want 09:21 Mind States And Emotion 15:09 Helpful Attention 20:05 Body-Based Practice 29:22 Allowing And Exploring 40:19 Letting Go Of Demands 45:14 Second Sitting 54:51 Object And Walking Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13853/

item.title: Guided Meditation - The Play of Appearances
item.description:
Beginning with the simple act of finding the seat, the talk guides attention into the body and then gently widens it into a spacious, tender receiving. Experience is repeatedly framed as something to be welcomed rather than managed: sensations, thoughts, self-images, and reactions are all treated as appearances arising in space. The movement deepens into softening, acknowledging, bowing, and letting go, until even sounds and mind-movements are felt as insubstantial and peace is named as the underlying nature of things.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the seat 02:15 Receiving and softening 06:18 Appearances in space 11:03 Welcoming selfing 15:54 Bowing to experience 20:27 Letting the heart release 26:24 Floating in space 32:47 Peace in all things
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12522/

item.title: Seventh Instructions and Guided Metta Meditation: Metta and Emptiness
item.description:
How can loving-kindness widen without becoming forced or abstract? The talk moves from the familiar metta categories—self, friend, difficult person, all beings—into practical ways of opening the practice through body sense, space, and phrasing, while keeping the questioning alive about what is actually helpful in any moment. From there it turns toward a subtler point: as the mind softens, perception changes, and things that felt solid can be seen as empty of inherent problemness. The final stretch returns to everyday habits like wanting to hunker down, go to bed, or stay in when it’s cold, asking whether those movements are kindness, aversion, or a closing of the heart.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Metta categories 02:53 Turning toward self 05:19 Methods for all beings 08:36 Space filled with love 13:20 Perception and emptiness 17:11 Samadhi and insight 19:16 Aversion closes the heart 24:11 Questioning habit 30:26 Return to practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12526/

item.title: Fifth Instructions and Guided Metta Meditation
item.description:
How do you widen metta beyond the people the heart already likes? The talk works through the move from easy categories to the neutral person, noting boredom, drift, and the need for extra care, then reframes the practice through shared humanity, vulnerability, mortality, and ordinary joy. It then settles into a guided meditation that begins with the body, uses simple phrases, and gradually invites a neutral person into the field of warm well-wishing without forcing the heart.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Introducing the neutral person 03:26 Shared humanity 08:50 Phrases and flexibility 12:13 Stretching the heart 15:46 Self, friend, and shadow 18:12 Settling into practice 27:17 Neutral person meditation 39:41 Closing and return
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12515/

item.title: Fourth Instructions and Guided Metta Meditation
item.description:
How do you keep metta alive when the body is comfortable one moment, difficult the next, and sometimes feels like nothing at all is happening? The talk keeps returning to that practical question, moving from a review of the "three baskets" of practice into a flexible way of working with bodily well-being, pain, and the normal absence of feeling. It then opens into a guided meditation that widens awareness through the whole body, using light, warmth, and simple imagery to support a spacious, gentle metta practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Reviewing the practice 08:17 Samadhi as spaciousness 12:59 Working with difficulty 23:01 Whole-body metta 28:23 Boundless in steps 30:09 Guided body meditation 41:05 Silent cultivation 50:24 Closing tone
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12519/

item.title: Second Instructions and Guided Metta Meditation
item.description:
How do you keep metta alive without turning it heavy, rigid, or forced? The talk moves through a practical answer: treat the practice like sailing, adjusting to the conditions; when things are smooth, stay with the warmth; when difficulty is present, include it with kindness; and when it is too much, know when to set the formal practice down and meet experience differently. A guided metta practice follows, using posture, bodily sensitivity, phrases, visualization, and the felt sense of warmth to let kindness gather around the whole body and then toward oneself and another person.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Responsive metta 01:29 The sailing analogy 04:47 Working with difficulty 10:30 Mindfulness with warmth 12:21 Phrases and resonance 19:02 Allowing well-being 26:23 Self and easiest person 33:23 Guided metta practice 48:29 Closing the practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12524/

item.title: First Instructions and Guided Metta Meditation
item.description:
Beginning with posture, breath, and a light sense of the heart, the talk guides a metta practice that moves from settling into the whole body to offering simple phrases of well-wishing. It then opens outward to an easy person, with suggestions for using words, images, or body-sense in whatever way is most natural. The speaker keeps returning to patience, flexibility, and the reminder that kindness is planted gradually rather than forced.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Entering the practice 04:32 Opening the field 10:11 Self-directed metta 19:10 Seeds and tenderness 26:23 The easiest person 34:04 Returning to openness 36:29 What metta means 40:10 Ways of practicing 48:04 Patience and walking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12516/

item.title: Liberation and the Self - A Guided Inquiry
item.description:
What if liberation is not a fixed idea to believe in, but something to examine in the body, the heart, and the mind? The talk begins by setting up a guided inquiry into what is operating inside us—beliefs, assumptions, fears, and hidden contractions—and then moves into a step-by-step exploration of a person’s relationship to freedom itself. From there, it asks three linked questions: what liberation means, how it affects practice and life, and how the self is quietly defined around it, all while returning to trust, kindness, and careful attention. The emphasis stays on noticing what is actually present and on seeing whether those self-views are true, helpful, or something that can soften.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 04:23 Inquiry as an art 11:43 How to work with it 15:49 First question: liberation 29:33 Second question: its effects 39:16 Third question: self-view 47:31 Letting go of locking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11968/

item.title: Metta Instructions and Guided Meditation 5
item.description:
The talk begins with a simple reassurance: metta is hard to get wrong, and the practice can unfold through many different emphases without being forced. From there, it moves through the body as a base for kindness, then through ways of working with discomfort, using imagination, posture, and even touch to keep the practice alive and flexible. Midway, the talk widens into the Metta Sutta and then into the felt sense that kindness can alter perception itself, until the guided practice opens outward to all beings, with repeated reminders that feeling anything particular is not required. The closing meditation settles into a spacious field of kindness, radiating gently in all directions.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Metta is forgiving 02:43 Working with difficulty 04:37 Care for the body 09:03 Energy and imagination 14:14 The Metta Sutta 16:44 How metta changes perception 23:42 Guided practice begins 25:56 Opening to all beings 34:12 Seeds without pressure
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9987/

item.title: Metta Instructions and Guided Meditation 4
item.description:
How do you keep loving-kindness from collapsing into heaviness when it meets suffering, conflict, or the difficult person? The talk keeps returning to balance: build buoyancy first, so compassion has strength, and then move gradually from the easy person to more painful relationships without forcing the heart. The guided meditation follows that arc, beginning with posture, softness, and bodily presence, then turning metta toward easier targets, later toward someone difficult, with permission to step back into self-kindness and to loosen the story around the relationship.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Balancing metta and compassion 02:18 Approaching difficult people 07:05 What to reflect on 09:58 Working with hurt and anger 11:44 Settling into meditation 25:13 Opening to a difficult person 31:02 Spacious, patient effort 39:14 Story and self-definition 43:21 Returning and carrying on
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9986/

item.title: Metta Instructions and Guided Meditation 3
item.description:
The talk begins with the difficulty of receiving love, and then turns that problem into practice by asking what it is to receive from nature, from other people, and even from one’s own metta. From there, the speaker introduces a gentle way of working with the neutral person: noticing common humanity, vulnerability, mortality, and the ways self-consciousness can block openness. The talk then moves into a guided mettā meditation, first with the easier figures and then with the neutral person, gradually widening the field of well-wishing and ending in a simple, open sensitivity to the body.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Receiving Kindness 08:28 Metta And Energy 11:14 Neutral Person Reflections 18:30 Settling For Practice 21:26 Self Metta And Easiest Person 32:08 Neutral Person Practice 35:52 Closing And Return
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9985/

item.title: Metta Instructions and Guided Meditation 2
item.description:
The talk keeps returning to one question: how do you stay gently with metta without forcing it? The speaker describes practice as something light, responsive, and flexible, using the image of sailing to show how one adjusts to conditions rather than pushing through them. He moves through practical guidance on phrases, bodily sensitivity, and the different ways metta can feel, then introduces the friend as the next object of practice and opens into a guided meditation. Imagination is treated as part of the method here, a way to let warmth, tenderness, and well-wishing take form.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the tone 03:42 Working with hindrances 06:36 Flexible practice forms 10:16 What metta can feel like 13:58 Body and resonance 19:32 Simple self and basics 24:21 Using imagination 32:49 Metta for a friend 43:04 Closing the practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9984/

item.title: Metta Instructions and Guided Meditation 1
item.description:
How do you practice metta when the feeling is absent, dry, or uneven? The talk moves from a clear explanation of self-kindness and the benefactor practice into a long guided meditation, with repeated phrases, body awareness, and optional visualization used as supports rather than goals. A Buddha quote is used to underline the central point that lovingkindness is not something to earn: there is no one more deserving of metta than oneself. The guidance stays practical and flexible, encouraging listeners to find what actually works in their own body and mind.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Metta and Boundlessness 03:05 Finding the Benefactor 05:05 Playful Practice 08:27 Working with Phrases 11:46 Feeling and Intention 16:41 Self Metta Guided 23:56 Self Kindness Continues 33:23 Turning to the Benefactor 46:55 Imagery and Walking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9983/

item.title: Guided Meditation: The Vastness of Awareness
item.description:
The practice begins with settling into the body, calming and gathering attention, and then gradually opening that steadiness into wide listening. From there, the talk turns toward the felt sense of awareness as spacious, clear, and undisturbed, with sounds, sensations, thoughts, and images all arising and passing like waves, reflections, clouds, or fireflies in an open sky. Again and again, the invitation is to let everything belong without clinging, rejecting, or trying to control the experience, so the listener can sense the imperturbable stillness underneath appearances.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Settling In 01:19 Body and Stillness 04:11 Opening to Sound 05:50 Silence as Ground 09:57 Sky of Awareness 14:15 Letting Everything Belong 18:43 Waves and Reflections 24:31 Repeated Refrain 36:06 Pervading Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9544/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Three Characteristics
item.description:
Impermanence is the starting point here: the practice keeps returning to the changing feel of sensations in the body, then in sounds and the visual field. From there, the talk moves through three characteristics in a very practical way—seeing pleasant and unpleasant as conditions for craving or aversion, allowing experience to be as it is, and gently unhooking the sense that anything is me or mine. The talk also pauses to distinguish this from chasing meditation experiences, and to place insight practice alongside calming practice as a flexible balance.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Settling In 04:43 Impermanence in the Body 10:09 Wider Field of Change 20:24 Reactivity and Dukkha 27:32 Letting Be 29:40 Not-Self Practice 39:00 Seeing Without Clinging 47:17 Balancing the Practice 53:16 Two Ways of Not-Self
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9543/

item.title: The Space of Awareness - Welcoming (4th Guided Meditation)
item.description:
What changes when the first move is not to fix experience, but to welcome it completely? The talk begins with posture, settling, and a simple wish for kindness, then turns into a sustained guided practice of opening to whatever is most present—body sensation, mood, sound, resistance, or ease. As it continues, the emphasis widens from a single prominent feeling to the whole field of awareness, including subtle tension, the image of self, and even the fading of experience itself, with repeated reminders to soften, allow, and let things be.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the posture 01:17 Setting kindness 02:37 Welcoming what arises 04:21 Opening the whole body 06:11 Including sounds and space 08:31 Resting in allowance 14:12 Returning to welcoming 27:57 Working with resistance 34:22 Welcoming subtlety
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9813/

item.title: The Space of Awareness - Only Impressions in Awareness (3rd Guided Meditation)
item.description:
The talk begins by acknowledging that the afternoon meditations may feel different, then quietly shows how they are all ways of entering the same territory. It moves from kindness and settling in the body, to sensing stillness, opening to sound and silence, and then widening into an image of awareness as open sky in which thoughts, sensations, and even self-images pass like clouds, bubbles, waves, or reflections. Again and again, the emphasis is on letting experience arise and fade without clinging or resistance, and on noticing the undisturbed space that already holds it all.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the retreat field 00:42 Settling with kindness 02:29 Sensing stillness 04:33 Listening into silence 08:04 Sky of awareness 12:13 Nothing to hold 22:23 Impressions in awareness 31:53 Self as appearance 35:31 Qualities and aftercare
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9820/

item.title: The Space of Awareness - Silence (2nd Guided Meditation)
item.description:
How does awareness open when it stops dividing experience into separate pieces? This guided sitting begins with posture, bodily poise, and kindness, then moves into listening to bare sound, the total field of hearing, and the felt contrast between openness and bright presence. From there it widens further to include body sensations, thoughts, and images, asking whether sound and silence can be sensed together, with silence as something that permeates and holds everything.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the posture 01:54 Kindness and grounding 03:21 Listening to sound 05:21 Global sound field 09:02 Sounds arising and passing 12:17 Body in awareness 15:27 Everything is included 24:33 Silence within sound 30:20 Belonging to silence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9817/

item.title: Breath Meditation (2nd Instructions/Guided Meditation)
item.description:
Starting from simple breath awareness, the talk keeps changing the center of practice: first below the navel, then on the right and left sides, then up through the heart, throat, middle of the head, crown, spine, and upper back. Rather than fixing one “correct” way to breathe, the instruction repeatedly asks what feels best in the whole body, using a spacious, balloon-like awareness that can expand around ease and around discomfort. In the second half, the talk turns more directly to working with pain or constriction—relaxing the rest of the body, widening attention, and experimenting with breath, posture, and imagination without forcing anything.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and setup 00:26 Posture and kindness 02:10 Whole-body awareness 03:18 Lower belly and right side 09:34 Midline and heart 15:59 Head and crown centers 21:55 Base and upper back 29:05 Review and possibilities 39:23 Working with discomfort
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9818/

Starting from simple breath awareness, the talk keeps changing the center of practice: first below the navel, then on the right and left sides, then up through the heart, throat, middle of the head, crown, spine, and upper back. Rather than fixing one “correct” way to breathe, the instruction repeatedly asks what feels best in the whole body, using a spacious, balloon-like awareness that can expand around ease and around discomfort. In the second half, the talk turns more directly to working with pain or constriction—relaxing the rest of the body, widening attention, and experimenting with breath, posture, and imagination without forcing anything.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and setup 00:26 Posture and kindness 02:10 Whole-body awareness 03:18 Lower belly and right side 09:34 Midline and heart 15:59 Head and crown centers 21:55 Base and upper back 29:05 Review and possibilities 39:23 Working with discomfort
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9818/

item.title: The Space of Awareness - Impermanence (1st Guided Meditation)
item.description:
How does awareness change when the field is opened wide enough to notice impermanence itself? This guided meditation starts with body sensations, then expands to include sound and even thoughts, returning again and again to the shifting, flickering texture of experience. The practice keeps the attention light and open, using the perception of change to support letting go rather than concentration on content.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the practice 02:14 Settling the body 05:06 Widening to the body 10:10 Including sound 14:11 Only the change 18:46 Working with difficulty 24:02 Everything in the field 28:56 Space and flicker 34:12 Letting go, closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9811/

item.title: Breath Meditation (1st Instructions and Guided Meditation)
item.description:
What if the breath is not something to control, but something to learn from? The talk begins with posture and a full-body settling, then moves through relaxing tension in the face, throat, shoulders, chest, and belly before opening into a wide awareness of the whole body. From there it develops a practical, exploratory method: breathing long or short as needed, noticing energy, ease, and comfort, and using the breath to nourish a steadier, brighter mind without pressure.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Settling the posture 04:49 Kindness and body space 13:47 Long breath, whole body 20:50 Choosing what helps 30:12 Why practice this way 39:03 Six practical reminders 43:29 Patience and adjustment
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9812/

item.title: Guided Meditation - Cittamatra
item.description:
The meditation begins by settling awareness into the body, letting calm gather through the breath or mettā, and then gradually opens into listening, stillness, and silence. From there, the practice widens into a sky-like awareness: sounds, sensations, thoughts, and even self-sense are invited to appear and fade without struggle, control, or rejection. Near the end, the talk links this spacious seeing to three characteristics practice, suggesting a way of sensing experience as impressions in awareness rather than solid things.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the body 02:17 Body and stillness 05:51 Sound and silence 08:25 Awareness as sky 11:02 Letting things be 25:55 Impressions in awareness 36:27 Waves on the ocean 41:36 Three characteristics and practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11125/

item.title: Guided Meditation on the Three Characteristics
item.description:
How do the three characteristics show up when attention moves through the body, sound, sight, and thought? The talk begins with settling into balance, then shifts into three linked practices: noticing impermanence, relaxing aversion or grasping, and seeing experience as not-self, not me, not mine. It keeps returning to simple direct instructions—hands, face, whole body, listening, opening the eyes, and even thoughts—so the listener can hear how the same inquiry changes across different sense doors. Near the end, the speaker also addresses the fear that can arise as experience becomes less solid, and suggests meeting that fear gently while also noticing the peace or spaciousness alongside it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Three approaches 01:20 Settling the mind 05:54 Impermanence in the hands 11:40 Face and whole body 15:15 Sound and relation 26:39 Allowing experience 30:04 Not-self in awareness 40:13 Walking through the day 47:55 Fear and familiarization
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11124/

item.title: Second Morning Instructions and Guided Meditation
item.description:
How do you stay sensitive to the breath without losing steadiness, and what do you do when discomfort, dullness, restlessness, or doubt starts to take over? The talk first reviews the basic retreat instructions—whole-body awareness, experimenting with the breath, balancing calm and energy, and using the reflective mind to ask what is actually helpful. It then moves into a long guided meditation that works through different points in the body, inviting breath awareness into places of ease, constriction, and subtle energy until the whole body can be felt as one field.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Reviewing the basics 02:29 Sensitivity and steadiness 06:05 Working with discomfort 08:40 Breath as a resource 11:18 Hindrances and discernment 16:32 Settling the posture 21:22 Breath through the body 31:28 Down the body and feet 40:28 Centering and spaciousness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11512/

item.title: First Morning Instructions and Guided Meditation
item.description:
This guided meditation begins with posture, relaxation, and a wide, delicate awareness that fills the whole body rather than narrowing onto the breath alone. From there, the talk explores how the breath can be adjusted in response to what the body needs: opening, energizing, soothing, or calming, without forcing anything. It then turns toward finding even a small place of comfort or ease and patiently nourishing that until it can gradually spread. The instructions keep returning to play, responsiveness, and contentment with whatever is actually helpful right now.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Settling the posture 04:24 Whole-body awareness 11:36 Working with the breath 23:58 Breath as nourishment 36:40 Play without forcing 40:41 Comfort that can spread
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11510/

item.title: Guided Meditation - Directing Love Towards dharmas
item.description:
This guided meditation begins by settling the body and then turning attention toward whatever is most present, whether sensation, emotion, thought, or mood. From there, the practice keeps returning to a simple movement: bathing experience in kindness, compassion, and complete welcoming, even when what is there is resistance, numbness, boredom, fear, or peace. Midway through, the emphasis broadens to include not only experience itself, but also the intention of kindness and the relationship we have to what arises. The meditation closes by asking whether anything exists independent of that relationship, leaving the listener with a quiet question about how experience is made and met.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the body 02:03 Meeting what stands out 06:27 Bathing experience in kindness 12:23 Letting precision soften 20:12 Nothing is exempt 26:04 Including self and intention 34:26 Relationship and inquiry
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11957/

item.title: Guided Meditation on Gratitude and Compassion
item.description:
What happens when gratitude is not just a feeling but a way of seeing the whole web of support behind a life? The guided meditation moves from settling the body into a long sequence of recollections: benefactors, teachers, parents, friends, difficult people, strangers, animals, plants, and the wider ecosystem, each time pairing gratitude with an awareness of suffering and a wish for peace. By the end, the practice expands into a sense of the body radiating compassion in all directions, until everything is held in that tenderness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the Body 01:48 Benefactor and Compassion 05:08 Many Who Have Helped 15:00 Parents and Early Care 19:06 Friends and Intimacy 23:04 Unknown Benefactors 29:35 Trade and Difficult People 36:28 Animals and Ecosystem 45:10 Practice Options and Close
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11952/

item.title: Third Samatha Instructions and Guided Meditation
item.description:
The talk starts with posture, then moves into a slow guided exploration of breath and awareness by body center: lower belly, solar plexus, heart, throat, head, spine, and back. Rather than treating this as a fixed method, the speaker keeps returning to experiment—what breath feels most easeful, where attention can settle, and how the whole body changes when awareness is placed in one spot. In the second half, the talk opens into questions about difficult emotion, the balance between samatha and vipassana, and practical advice for adapting the practice when the breath is blocked or the body feels constricted.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the Posture 01:24 Lower Belly Center 07:05 Right Side and Belly 14:19 Solar Plexus and Heart 18:49 Throat to Crown 28:32 Spine and Return 31:59 After the Sit 37:18 Questions and Context 46:08 Closing Instructions
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12314/

item.title: Second Samatha Instructions and Guided Meditation
item.description:
How do you work with the breath so it becomes both energizing and calming, without getting pulled into strain, dullness, or judgment? The talk gathers together samatha instructions around starting with a long, comfortable breath, then sensing when the body wants a different rhythm—shorter, subtler, or even still—and keeping a full awareness of the whole body throughout. It also looks at common obstacles like hindrances, emotional tightness, pleasantness, and pain, offering simple ways to respond: soften pressure, brighten attention, stay with what is okay, or move the body when needed. After the instruction, the talk shifts into a long guided meditation, and later includes a stretch of quoted literary and teacher references, with Rainer Maria Rilke recurring as a strong influence.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Gathering the instructions 05:25 Balancing energy and calm 08:39 Working with hindrances 10:53 Turning toward pleasantness 13:12 Meeting sitting pain 14:43 Posture and settling 17:03 Guided breath practice 27:27 Influences and recitations
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12313/

item.title: First Samatha Instructions and Guided Meditation on the Breath
item.description:
What if the real work of breath practice is not how many breaths you can count, but how alive and kind the mind becomes while it keeps returning? The talk sets samatha inside a larger view of the path: cultivating calm, patience, non-judgment, and a bright attentiveness that can support insight. A guided meditation then moves through posture, relaxing tension, and sensing the breath in the whole body, gradually shifting from long, slow breathing to a more subtle search for what feels most comfortable and enjoyable. By the end, the emphasis is on making the attention clear, spacious, and sensitive enough to include even discomfort without tightening around it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 The bigger picture 02:26 Why practice with breath 04:44 Patience over self-judgment 10:06 Quality, not counting 12:22 Settling into the body 25:38 Whole-body breathing 34:14 Adjusting the breath 39:29 Bright and alive
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12310/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Metta to phenomena
item.description:
What happens when metta is turned away from people and toward the raw phenomena of experience itself? The talk guides a steady practice of noticing whatever is most prominent in consciousness—body sensations, thoughts, emotions, states of mind—and bathing it in kindness, compassion, and warmth. It also shifts into a more receptive mode of complete welcoming, where precision matters less than an open, tender holding of whatever arises, including resistance, numbness, fear, or tiredness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Turning Toward Experience 01:28 Bathing the Moment 04:07 Holding with Metta 07:30 Open Reception 09:51 Softening Repeatedly 13:47 Less Precision, More Kindness 18:07 Welcoming All States 20:58 Long Receptive Silence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12292/
